skill.name: agent-tools
skill.description: Use the inference.sh belt CLI to discover and run cloud AI apps for images, video, LLMs, search, 3D, and Twitter/X automation.
skill.summary: Run inference.sh AI apps from assistant workflows using the belt CLI.
skill.author: infsh-skills

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell, network, filesystem, and credential patterns. Most are documentation examples for the inference.sh belt CLI, but the skill legitimately enables remote execution, local file upload, and social posting workflows. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found, so this is publishable with elevated-risk warnings.

medium_findings.title: Pipe-to-Shell Installer Instruction
medium_findings.description: The documentation recommends piping a remote installer from cli.inference.sh into sh. This can execute network-delivered code on the user machine, even though the source appears to be the vendor installer and an alternative manual path is provided.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky execution pattern is explicit and repeated. Confidence is below very high because it is installation documentation for the named CLI, not hidden or malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Broad Remote AI Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to run belt app commands against many cloud services, including image, video, LLM, search, and Twitter/X apps. These commands send prompts and task inputs to external services and can perform account actions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples and category table clearly show cloud execution and social automation. The behavior is core functionality, so it is risky but not evidence of abuse.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Local File Uploads
medium_findings.description: The skill states that local paths are automatically uploaded when passed as inputs. This can expose local images, audio, video, screenshots, or other files if an agent selects sensitive paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly describes automatic upload behavior and gives absolute, relative, and home path examples. The data exposure risk depends on user consent and path selection.

medium_findings.title: API Key Handling Guidance
medium_findings.description: The references instruct users to set INFSH_API_KEY and note local credential storage. This is normal for CLI authentication, but it increases impact if prompts or logs expose environment values.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential references are explicit and legitimate. The concern is operational handling of secrets rather than evidence of exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Shell Examples Flagged as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Many static external-command findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences and inline command examples. They are user-facing CLI examples, not embedded Ruby code or hidden execution logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are Markdown references with visible command snippets. There is no executable Ruby source in the reviewed locations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: Static URL findings point to inference.sh documentation, installer, image, and result URLs. These are expected for a CLI integration skill and do not show unauthorized data transfer by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation or expected platform endpoints. Network risk remains because belt sends user data, but the hardcoded links alone are low severity.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Text Found
low_findings.description: Targeted review found no instructions that ask the evaluator to ignore prior instructions, override system policy, skip security analysis, or claim pre-approval.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Searches and manual review of the skill and reference files did not reveal prompt-injection language. This is a negative finding with confidence limited to the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The pattern curl -fsSL followed by sh is present in install and reinstall instructions. Users should prefer the documented manual install and checksum verification path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is directly present. It is not hidden, but it is a well-known risky installer workflow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Path Inputs Become Remote Uploads
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill explains that file paths in app inputs are uploaded automatically. This pattern needs user confirmation before sending local files to remote AI apps.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The upload behavior is clearly documented and central to the examples. The security impact is real if sensitive local paths are used.

dangerous_patterns.title: Social Account Automation Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples include posting to Twitter/X and list related social actions. These commands can create public or account-affecting actions through the connected account.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The text explicitly lists posting and automation commands. The risk is clear, although use appears intentional and user-facing.

content.value_statement: AI app selection and execution can be hard to coordinate from an assistant. This skill gives CLI workflows for inference.sh apps, files, tasks, and authentication.
actual_capabilities: Installs and authenticates the inference.sh belt CLI.
Lists, searches, filters, and inspects available inference.sh apps.
Runs AI apps with inline input or saved input files.
Tracks long-running tasks and saves task outputs.
Uploads local media paths for supported app inputs.
Shows examples for image, video, LLM, search, 3D, and Twitter/X apps.
limitations: Requires an inference.sh account and authenticated CLI session.
Cloud app availability, pricing, and quotas are controlled by inference.sh.
Local files may be uploaded when used as app inputs.
Some workflows can post to external accounts and need user approval.

use_cases.title: Prototype Multimodal AI Outputs
use_cases.description: Generate images, videos, speech, and 3D assets from assistant-guided CLI commands.

use_cases.title: Run Cloud Models from Scripts
use_cases.description: Call LLM, search, and media apps from repeatable terminal workflows without managing GPUs.

use_cases.title: Manage AI Task Results
use_cases.description: Submit long-running jobs, check task status, and save generated outputs for later review.

prompt_templates.title: Find an App
prompt_templates.prompt: Find inference.sh apps for image generation and explain which one fits a fast concept mockup.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Simple Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the inference.sh CLI to run an image generation app with this prompt: a clean studio product photo.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare File Input
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a safe plan to run an image-to-video app using my local image, including what file will be uploaded.

prompt_templates.title: Automate a Long Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Submit a long video generation job without waiting, record the task ID, then show me how to fetch the result later.

output_examples.input: Find image generation options for a fast product concept.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists matching inference.sh apps, compares speed and quality, and recommends one app with the exact CLI command to inspect it.

output_examples.input: Run a video generation job and do not wait for completion.
output_examples.output: The assistant submits the job, reports the task ID, and gives a follow-up command to check status and save the result.

output_examples.input: Use my local audio file for an avatar video.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the upload risk, asks for confirmation of the file path, and prepares the app run command only after approval.

best_practices: Confirm the target app, account, cost, and destination before running remote commands.
Use sample input files for complex apps so parameters can be reviewed before execution.
Ask before uploading local files or posting content to Twitter/X.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote installers into a shell without reviewing the source or using manual verification.
Do not pass broad local paths or sensitive files as app inputs.
Do not run social posting commands without explicit user approval.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps assistants use the inference.sh belt CLI to discover apps, run jobs, and manage outputs.

faq.question: Does it require an account?
faq.answer: Yes. The CLI needs login or an INFSH_API_KEY for authenticated use.

faq.question: Can it upload local files?
faq.answer: Yes. The CLI can upload local paths when an app input accepts files or URLs.

faq.question: Can it post to Twitter/X?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples include Twitter/X apps, so users should approve any account action first.

faq.question: Is the installer risk-free?
faq.answer: No. The docs include a pipe-to-shell installer, and the safer manual install path should be preferred.

faq.question: Which assistants can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
